skill.name: ace-step
skill.description: Generate, inpaint, and outpaint music with ACE Step models through the RunComfy CLI. The skill routes text-to-audio, multilingual lyric generation, time-range repair, and track extension requests to supported RunComfy endpoints.
skill.summary: Create and edit ACE Step music through RunComfy CLI workflows.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis heavily flagged Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell execution, but those hits are documentation and code examples, not executable Ruby. Real risk remains because the skill intentionally invokes the networked runcomfy CLI, sends prompts, lyrics, and audio URLs to RunComfy, and documents local token storage. No evidence found of malicious intent or prompt injection text in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Networked CLI Sends User Content to RunComfy
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to run the runcomfy CLI against RunComfy model endpoints. Prompts, lyrics, tags, and source audio URLs can leave the local environment, so users need clear consent and data handling expectations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool and examples directly invoke runcomfy run, and the text states the CLI posts to the RunComfy Model API. This is intended behavior, but it is a real outbound data flow.

medium_findings.title: API Token Stored in User Configuration
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that runcomfy login writes an API token under the user configuration directory and supports RUNCOMFY_TOKEN. This is normal CLI behavior, but mishandling the token could expose credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly names token storage and an environment variable token path. It also says the file uses mode 0600, which reduces but does not remove credential-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party Audio URLs Are Untrusted Inputs
medium_findings.description: Inpaint and outpaint workflows require HTTPS audio URLs, and the skill warns that source audio is untrusted. This creates privacy and indirect prompt-influence concerns when users provide external media.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow requires remote audio URLs and the security section identifies these URLs as untrusted. The impact depends on the audio source and user workflow, so medium severity is appropriate.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer reported many Ruby backtick execution findings, but SKILL.md is Markdown documentation. The flagged backticks are inline code spans, fenced bash examples, or literal CLI names rather than Ruby code executing at skill load time.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of SKILL.md shows Markdown prose and command examples, not a Ruby source file. The real command risk is separately captured as networked runcomfy CLI execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Point to Documented Service Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URLs are present, but they point to RunComfy home, model catalog, CLI documentation, or skills.sh references. No evidence found of covert callbacks, unrelated telemetry, or suspicious exfiltration domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs match the stated RunComfy documentation and catalog purpose. They are still useful evidence of a network dependency, but not evidence of malicious network behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings do not correspond to cryptographic code in SKILL.md. The referenced lines are YAML description text, music prompt examples, or comparison tables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain no hash functions, cipher choices, key handling, or cryptographic implementation. The scanner appears to have matched ordinary text tokens.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Static Hits Are Documentation Text
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance findings occur in endpoint selection prose, not commands that inspect the host system. No evidence found of uname, host fingerprinting, process listing, or environment inventory behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines say when to avoid specific music endpoints. They do not contain host reconnaissance commands or instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Allowed Bash Tool for runcomfy Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill declares Bash(runcomfy *) and provides runcomfy run examples. This should remain constrained to runcomfy commands only.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed-tools declaration and scope statement directly define Bash access. The command scope is narrow, but still executes an external CLI.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Output Directory Writes
dangerous_patterns.description: The command examples write generated audio to local output directories. This is expected, but it gives the skill a filesystem write path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Every run example uses an output directory for downloaded audio files. The paths are local and user-visible, so this is expected but should be disclosed.

content.user_title: Generate and Edit ACE Step Music
content.value_statement: Music generation workflows often need clear model routing, repeatable inputs, and low-cost iteration. This skill helps Claude, Codex, or Claude Code run ACE Step generation, inpainting, and outpainting through RunComfy.
actual_capabilities: Routes music requests to ACE Step base, ACE Step 1.5, audio-inpaint, or audio-outpaint endpoints.
Builds RunComfy CLI commands for tag-driven text-to-audio generation.
Supports structured lyrics with verse, chorus, bridge, and instrumental markers.
Guides time-range audio repair using start and end timestamps.
Guides track extension before, after, or around an existing audio file.
Explains cost, duration, endpoint selection, and common CLI exit codes.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI to be installed and authenticated before use.
Sends prompts, lyrics, tags, and source audio URLs to RunComfy services.
Each ACE Step call is limited to about four minutes of generated audio.
Inpaint and outpaint workflows require accessible HTTPS audio URLs.

use_cases.title: Draft Background Tracks
use_cases.description: Create low-cost instrumental loops or background tracks from genre, mood, instrument, and tempo tags.

use_cases.title: Localize Vocal Concepts
use_cases.description: Use ACE Step 1.5 to test the same song structure with lyrics in multiple languages.

use_cases.title: Repair or Extend Existing Audio
use_cases.description: Regenerate a selected section or add an intro and outro around a short musical hook.

prompt_templates.title: Generate an Instrumental Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ACE Step to create a 60 second instrumental track. Style: lo-fi hip-hop, mellow, soft drums, electric piano, 75 BPM. Save it to a local output folder.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Structured Vocal Song
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a 90 second ACE Step 1.5 vocal track with verse, chorus, bridge, and outro sections. Use indie pop tags and keep the lyrics suitable for commercial review.

prompt_templates.title: Replace a Song Section
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ACE Step audio-inpaint to replace seconds 30 through 50 of this user-provided track URL. Match the original style and make the section instrumental.

prompt_templates.title: Extend a Short Hook
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ACE Step audio-outpaint to add a 20 second intro and 45 second outro to this hook URL. Preserve the tempo and build naturally into the chorus.

output_examples.input: Create a calm 60 second instrumental for a product demo.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects ACE Step base, prepares mellow instrumental tags, uses an instrumental lyric marker, and writes the generated audio to the chosen output folder.

output_examples.input: Fix the bridge between 40 and 65 seconds in my uploaded track.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects audio-inpaint, keeps the edit window bounded by the requested timestamps, and asks for a user-approved HTTPS audio URL if one is missing.

output_examples.input: Turn this 30 second hook into a longer version with an intro and ending.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects audio-outpaint, adds before and after durations, and describes the extension style using tags that match the source material.

best_practices: Confirm the user wants to send prompts, lyrics, and audio URLs to RunComfy before running the CLI.
Use specific tags for genre, mood, instruments, tempo, vocal style, and arrangement.
Set a seed when the user needs reproducible variations or reviewable iterations.
anti_patterns: Do not use copyrighted lyrics unless the user confirms they have rights.
Do not run installer commands from unreviewed remote scripts.
Do not process third-party audio URLs that the user did not explicitly provide for the task.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It helps an assistant generate, repair, or extend music with ACE Step models through the RunComfy CLI.

faq.question: Does it require a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The CLI must be installed and authenticated with runcomfy login or a RUNCOMFY_TOKEN environment variable.

faq.question: Can it create multilingual songs?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends ACE Step 1.5 for multilingual lyrics and structured vocal sections.

faq.question: Can it edit an existing track?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide time-range inpainting and before-or-after outpainting when the user provides an HTTPS audio URL.

faq.question: What data leaves my environment?
faq.answer: The CLI can send prompts, lyrics, tags, audio URLs, and model inputs to RunComfy services.

faq.question: Why is the security rating medium?
faq.answer: The skill has no confirmed malicious behavior, but it runs a networked CLI, handles tokens, and writes generated files locally.
